speaker: interviewer
text: Today is January 2nd, 1997. I'm interviewing Shirley Warman, whose name at birth was Niske. My name is Mildred Gelfand, G-E-L-F-A-N-D. This is Brooklyn, New York, USA. The language spoken is English. Today is January 2nd, 1997. I'm interviewing Shirley Warman, whose last name at birth was Niske. My name is Mildred Gelfand, G-E-L-F-A-N-D. This is Brooklyn, New York, USA. The language spoken is English. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Please say your name and spell it.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Shirley Warman, W-A-R-M-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you spell your first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: S-H-R-Y-L-Y.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell it again?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think it's right. S-H-R-Y. No, S-H-R-L-Y.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is this the same name you were given at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My name at birth was Sura.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was Niske.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell both names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Sura, S-U-R-A. Niske, N-I-S-N-E.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell your last name again? Your maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Niske. N-E-S-K-E.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you known by any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: My birth date is March the 3rd, 1919.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old does that make you now?

speaker: interviewee
text: 78. I'm going to be 78.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lublin. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: What country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that the address that you lived at?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what part of Poland is Lublin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lublin is central Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you remember Lublin before World War II?

speaker: interviewee
text: With Jewish communities, beautiful city, a beautiful park, and [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] was a lot of-- you know, a lot of young people belonged to organizations. And that's it. I mean, you know, I was young and I don't--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the city look?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beautiful. Was an old city. Lublin is a very religious, old city.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there are a lot of synagogues there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there was. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many houses did they have or how were the houses built?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very big houses, brick houses. Brick. There was houses, you know, three flights up, or five flights up, one flight. Everyone was brick houses.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there Jewish theaters there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There was a Jewish theater.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it called?

speaker: interviewee
text: They used to come from Russia, the actors, to play.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, a couple of times I went. I have to have my parents' permission to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there Jewish police stations there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any of the Jewish leaders in the community?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was in senate, you know, like over here, there was Bella Shapiro. She was a Jewish one. And one doctor. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't remember his second name.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the function of the Jewish community leaders?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was like over here in senate. You know. That's it. I don't know much about that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had very lovely parents. My mother was 40 years old. My father was 42. And there was a beautiful marriage. My mother didn't work. She had a six children. My father had a store for materials.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kuna. My mother's name was Laia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tannebaum.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were they born?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's Poland. But the outskirt from Lublin.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your parents like?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- you know, it was a nice family. Everything was beautiful. We always-- you know, a whole week, nobody was home for dinner together, but that comes Friday night and Saturday, after shul, all the family sit together by the table. My mother prepare a lot of food. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did she prepare? What type of food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gefilte fish, chicken soup with noodles, and some meat-- chicken. For dessert, we had cookies and apple sauce.

speaker: interviewer
text: And all six children?

speaker: interviewee
text: All six of them [INAUDIBLE] at separate table. Friday night and Saturday [INAUDIBLE] after shul.

speaker: interviewer
text: With your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you discuss at the table at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot of things. You know, my father was very religious. My older brother, Joseph, was a religious boy and they were in different places. And I started belong, you know, to the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE]. This was an organization, just for fun, with a lot of girls, lot of boys. And then I switch-- I switched to the Bund.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the difference between both of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, both of them was organizations for the workers. But I choose them, but I don't know. This one was for Israel, the Zionist, and this one, the Bund, was to make better for the workers in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: What made you switch?

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot of girls, a lot of friends of mine, and they did, and they told me, and we went together. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your brothers and sisters, can you tell me who they were, their names and ages?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My-- my oldest brother, he was Joseph. He was-- this time or now?

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was born 1913. How old was he? Till '39, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, tell me about Joseph.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he-- he was-- he was in Polish army. And then, you know, in Poland, you-- I think it was a year and a half or two. And then he came [INAUDIBLE] home, and he was study for rabbi. He met a girl, a very nice one, and they got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shaindel. They never had children before the war broke out. And my-- the other brother, Simcha, he-- he went more with me. He was playing in an orchestra. He was in the wartime-- he was in the army too.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was-- I don't know how you call it-- on the horses, you know to ride? It's-- And he worked-- he was a captain. He never got married. And he belonged together with me in the same organization. And who was after?

speaker: interviewee
text: After? Was-- I am.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after you?

speaker: interviewee
text: After me was-- I forgot her name already-- was-- I think it was Masha. I think so. I don't know. Masha. She was still in school. She was 13 years old. And my father made everybody should study very hard. And we talk a lot about life, and this was going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there are any other children after Marsha.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Was another two girls. One was Riv-- Rivka. She was young. She was in school. And after her was [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [? Devorah, ?] I think. Yeah, Dora. [? Devorah. ?] In Poland, it's [? Devorah. ?] And she was very young.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would you say about your relationship with your sisters and brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had very good relationship with everyone. It's a family, but you have to have good relationship to go on with life. Life was very hard, but we had it very good. My father was a very big provider. I didn't work till I got married. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said your father had a fabric store?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he sell there, exactly?

speaker: interviewee
text: Materials. And sometimes my older brother went in to help him out. And my grandpa-- my father's parents used to live with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my grandfather's name was Froim Yitzchak.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Niske. And my grandma's-- grandma's name-- [? Shifla ?] or something? I don't remember. [? Shifla. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your grandparents.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not much to tell about them, you know? My grandfather was an old man already, and [INAUDIBLE] she was like this. And when the war broke out, he died after three years.

speaker: interviewer
text: He died, you know? He had his [INAUDIBLE]. How do you call that? That's right. And he died. And I-- and my grandma was-- she went back to Parczew. She had a big family there. How long did she live with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they stayed with us five years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they very orthodox?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same thing like my father. Yeah. They had a kosher home.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of involvement in religious activities did your family have?

speaker: interviewee
text: There's not much involvement. They went-- you know, every day, every morning, they went, you know, to daven, like this, you know, over there. And in the evening, the same thing. And Friday night, they went to shul. And Saturday, in the morning, they went to shul.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any holidays or religious celebrations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there's a lot of holidays and religious celebration. There's Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur, then we celebrate Purim, and Passover.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your favorite?

speaker: interviewee
text: For me, it was everything good. We had a-- a lovely home. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your home.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very lovely. My-- my mother used to love my friends. They used to come in, and my brother brought friends in. And a lot of friends with school went together. We had-- we had picnics. We went to the beach. But all the time, I had to have my parents' permission. This-- like this we brought up.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you, before the war, when you needed your parents' permission?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- I was 16. It was New Years' Eve and all my friends went to-- to-- [INAUDIBLE] to organization, and they had a dance. And I asked my mother and my father if I could go, and I told them with whom I'm going. And he said I cannot go. I start to cry, and my father went to bed. But my mother said, OK, your father is going to sleep. You could sneak out. But you've got to be home before-- if he were to wake up, you see, you are in bed. I did it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What time did he tell you-- did your mother tell you to be home by?

speaker: interviewee
text: 11:00. See, but at 11:00, all the doors, they was locked. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You have to have-- you have to have-- there was a bell. You ring the bell, and the super came out and opened the door for you. You can't-- you didn't have no keys to go into the house-- to the building. Not to the house, to the building. We used to live on the third floor, of walking up. There was no elevators. And I was waiting downstairs and I said, OK. I was waiting downstairs a long time till the super came up and opened the door and my father showed me the key and I came in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was up.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he said, for about two weeks, you cannot go out nowhere. He slapped my face. He said, you didn't obey me, and this cannot be in this house. I start to cry, and I went to bed, and for two weeks, I couldn't go see my friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you react to that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was very angry. Very angry. But my mother said, well, you took it on your own. It-- it's your fault. But I said, why can they go and not me? I said, you don't live with them, you live with your father.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were his reasons for not letting you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really don't know. That's all. I don't know. I have no-- I have nothing. No attention. I don't know what the reason was. They were very strict. If you go out Saturday night, you got to tell them what time you come home. You got to be there. He said, it's for your own good. Some day, you're going to say, my father was right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, in looking back now, what do you say?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was right. But I couldn't do it with my children here. But I used to stay in the window, watch when they came in, and I make believe that I'm into the bed to sleep.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your father different with your brothers-- your older brothers-- than he was with the girls?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had love-- it didn't matter. Everyone had the same love, all the child, the oldest to the youngest.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said you had a very lovely home.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: In this time, it was beautiful. We had three rooms, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and we had a beautiful-- see, over here, the living room, over there, the beds was in the-- in the living room. It was a lot of pictures, and we had beautiful furniture in the house. And then was another room where we sit and eat for supper, and a very beautiful kitchen. And there were six children with a grandfather and grandmother. We were eight, 10 people. And a lot of people were sleeping on the floor too. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: You all slept in one room?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My father and my mother slept in the big bed, then my two brothers slept in the side beds. I was sleeping with my sisters, and my grandparents would sleep in the kitchen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any privacy there in the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Look, this was it. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were your neighbors?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish people. They were very nice neighbors. Yeah. No complaint [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember anybody? Their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was a long time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I finished public school, seventh grade.

speaker: interviewer
text: What ages did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: I started seven till 14.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about Jewish education?

speaker: interviewee
text: The rabbi came to the house. He taught me how to daven, when to-- you know, just like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your public school education, what did you learn in public school? What subjects did you study?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything. Everything. Everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like history, geography, and Polish, and writing, reading. You know, one hour a week, we had religious-- a rabbi came in, a teacher, and taught us, you know, Jewish things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your teachers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your classmates?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just one, but, you know, what's her name? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She was hiding me. See? I'm blank. I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there non-Jew-- non-Jewish children in the school as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we went together, Jewish and non-Jewish. There were Jewish schools separately, but I went with them together.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your relationship with the non-Jewish children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very good. No. Was no problem. There was no problem.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in the city of Lublin, what was the relationship between the Jews and non-Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was OK. You know? Not-- not too bad. Not too bad. The problems start in 1938. This already-- was already, you know. The Polish, and they start to beat up the Jewish girls, boys. But we didn't pay attention.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you said they started to beat up.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, like you went to the park and they start to start with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who started?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Polish ones. The Polish. Polish boys and girls, they start to beat-- you know, they start to fight the Jewish ones.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever beaten?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anybody in your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other incidents of antisemitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed away. When I saw a group, I moved away.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they do when they beat somebody up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just for fun. Just for fun.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they say anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I say, you're Jewish, you don't belong in this park. Go someplace else. That's it. Then I said, why shouldn't I? This park belongs to me like it belongs to you. Then they start fight.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you or your family know about the rise of Nazism in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know anything until the war broke out, and we see a lot of Jewish people came from Germany. Then we find out.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did the war began in Lublin? World War II?

speaker: interviewee
text: September, I think. I don't--

speaker: interviewer
text: Of what year?

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was not even 19 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you when the war broke out?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- I was in my home.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what do you remember about the German invasion?

speaker: interviewee
text: They start to bomb. You know, the bombs were falling. And my-- my little hou-- room was right away demolished. As I moved into my parents' house. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you living at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the war broke out?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was living with my parents. And I got married in my parents' house.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you married?

speaker: interviewee
text: In December, 1939, the 25th.

speaker: interviewer
text: To get back to the German invasion, you said your room was bombed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. You know, the bed-- from our apartment, the police station was, you know, one wall to the other. And a whole-- a half of our apartment got bombed. We went downstairs to my-- to the super.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was there?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had an apartment and they let us in to stay there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your reactions to this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was very bad. We walked out without anything. I was wearing just a night gown, a pair of slippers, and a bath robe.

speaker: interviewer
text: What time of day or night was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in the middle of the night. The date I don't remember. Middle of the night.

speaker: interviewer
text: In terms of the city of Lublin, what did the bombings do to the city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, a lot of demolished houses. And everybody start to roam. They didn't know where to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the city after the bombings?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very bad. Very bad. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were people hurt?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, a lot of people got hurt. You know, and that's-- everybody went their places-- you know, kept their places. There was-- everybody was afraid. They were just sticking to their family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the Jews make any attempts to help themselves at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: They couldn't. What could they do? They couldn't do anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to you and your family after the Germans took over?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. We were staying at the house. You couldn't go out. You could go out till 5:00. And the lights was big. There was no food any more. That's it. The windows-- the windows have to be shut. We put-- you know, they shut the windows. The lights shouldn't go out. Just-- you-- there was no-- you don't-- you didn't have to have lights to show if it's lights in the house. But they had electricity.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there German troops on the streets?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe how they treated the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't treat them-- nothing. That just was the beginning. Afterwards maybe, but now, at the beginning, they didn't treat-- they didn't say anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: People were able to live in their apartments?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they assaulted or punished or were there atrocities?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the beginning, no. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you taken out of your home or your parents taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. I was taken out in the-- I was-- you know, with the girls, I was taken out in a place, and there was hundreds and hundreds of people.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you taken out? Can you describe that?

speaker: interviewee
text: They just came in and they said I have to go. And I went, without a question.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was in the house at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents. They all was in the house and they took me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any reason that they took you?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was no reason at all. My mother start to cry. She was thinking they're going to kill me. But after three days, I came back home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did they take you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a big place, you know, very big. I don't remember. It was in the city, but very far. And then, they let us go, and I was running home.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they take you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: They have machine-- you know, the trucks. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And these were only women that were taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Then, another place was men. We weren't together. The men with the woman, they wasn't together.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened when you got to that place?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. They just-- they just, you know, walk around. We couldn't do anything. And they picked up 10 girls, and I was with them together. And they put us near the wall, and big, big dogs-- very big dogs that was standing near us. And there was the Germans standing at the other side. And they said, the Jews, you people, you gotta die. And we were standing, and they start to shoot. We all fell down, and we was laying. Then I felt something like my heart start to pump. And I moved and I moved and I was full of blood. And I see that all nine of them were dead. I touched them, and I start to run. I run home. And I came home. Everybody was crying, and my mother start to wash me up and to change my clothes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you hurt?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know if I hurt. I don't know. Emotionally, I didn't know what to say and I couldn't talk for a little while. Then I told her what happened. Then the sec-- the following day, I went down and I saw my friend.

speaker: interviewer
text: She was a Polish girl, and she said, you know, it happened just to you. Why don't you come and I put you in the basement? You're going to stay there. I'm going to hide you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, OK. I went down. And she'd been upstairs to my-- my parents' house every day. She brought me some food. And then, when she came down, she was sitting with me and she said to me, just like this-- if I would have the power, I would tell the Germans where all the Jews are.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, what are you talking about? I'm Jewish too. So she said, I don't mean you. I said, thank you very much and I went up to my parents' house. We'll continue on the next tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
